subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement 1 sounds plausible since exchange rates do fluctuate, but external liabilities are reported at current rates. Statement 4 is correct and often overlooked by candidates who assume defence or subsidies are the largest component.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is false (external liabilities use current exchange rates, not historical), eliminating options A, B, D; only option C remains with statements 2, 3, 4.
factual_hooks: External liabilities are reported at current exchange rates, not historical
Interest payments are the single largest component of non-plan revenue expenditure
High fiscal deficit crowds out private investment (crowding-out effect)
contrast_with: Revenue deficit vs Fiscal deficit — revenue deficit excludes capital borrowings
contemporary_relevance: Fiscal consolidation and interest payment burden remain key concerns in India's Union Budget.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Candidates may be unsure whether Archbishop Desmond Tutu won the Nobel Peace Prize (he did, 1984), leading to elimination of option A. All four won the Nobel Peace Prize, making option D correct.
elimination_hint: All four — Tutu (1984), Walesa (1983), Peres (1994), Arafat (1994) — are Nobel Peace Prize laureates; option D is correct.
factual_hooks: Desmond Tutu won Nobel Peace Prize 1984 for anti-apartheid work
Lech Walesa won 1983 for Solidarity movement in Poland
Peres and Arafat shared 1994 prize for Oslo Accords
contrast_with: Nobel Peace Prize vs Nobel Prize for Literature — different categories

subject: History
trap_factor: Garo and Kukis are also Northeast tribes with British contact, making elimination difficult without specific knowledge of the 1765 Diwani grant and the subsequent Bengal-Assam expansion route.
elimination_hint: The Khasis of the Meghalaya hills were the first hill tribe encountered as British expanded northeastward from Bengal after 1765 Diwani grant.
factual_hooks: Diwani of Bengal granted to East India Company in 1765
Khasi tribe was the first hill tribe the British encountered post-Diwani
contrast_with: Permanent Settlement (1793) vs Diwani grant (1765) — different landmarks of British revenue control

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement 1 seems plausible since Indian Railways is large, but the US system is actually larger. Statement 3 naming Kerala as highest road density is plausible but it is actually correct for some metrics, creating confusion.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is false (Indian Railways is among the largest but US is largest); eliminate options with 1. Statement 4 (NH7 as longest) and Statement 2 (NH caters 45%) are correct, pointing to option D.
factual_hooks: NH7 (now NH44) was the longest national highway in India at the time
National Highways cater to about 40-45% of road transport demand
contrast_with: State Highways vs National Highways — jurisdiction and funding differ
contemporary_relevance: Bharatmala Project and highway expansion remain key infrastructure priorities.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: The match-list format with four women and four achievements makes elimination from one correct pair difficult; Mira Nair's Golden Lion connection to Venice Film Festival is the most well-known anchor.
elimination_hint: Mira Nair winning the Golden Lion at Venice (for Monsoon Wedding) is a well-known fact; match C-4, then use process of elimination for remaining pairs.
factual_hooks: Mira Nair won the Golden Lion at Venice Film Festival for Monsoon Wedding (2001)
Neerja Bhanot Award is given for bravery
contrast_with: Golden Lion (Venice) vs Palme d'Or (Cannes) — different film festivals

subject: Governance
trap_factor: The exact prices under Antyodaya Anna Yojana (wheat Rs.2, rice Rs.3) are counterintuitive since rice is more expensive to produce; candidates may swap these figures.
elimination_hint: All three statements were correct at the time of the question (2002); the AAY prices (wheat Rs.2/kg, rice Rs.3/kg) and NOAPS Rs.75/month are specific figures to remember.
factual_hooks: Antyodaya Anna Yojana: wheat at Rs.2/kg, rice at Rs.3/kg for poorest families
National Old Age Pension Scheme provided Rs.75/month as central contribution
contrast_with: AAY vs BPL ration — AAY targets poorest of BPL households
contemporary_relevance: Evolved into National Food Security Act 2013; PMGKAY continues subsidised grain distribution.

subject: History
trap_factor: Kabir is often associated with Hindi due to his Dohas in common parlance, but Ramananda preceded him and was the first to use Hindi. Tulsidas also wrote in Awadhi (a Hindi dialect), creating confusion.
elimination_hint: Ramananda (14th-15th century) predates Kabir and Tulsidas and is credited as the first Bhakti saint to use Hindi (Brajbhasha/vernacular) for propagation, making option C correct.
factual_hooks: Ramananda was the first Bhakti saint to use Hindi for propagation
Kabir was a disciple of Ramananda
Tulsidas wrote Ramcharitmanas in Awadhi
contrast_with: Ramananda (Hindi) vs Ramanuja (Sanskrit) — different linguistic traditions in Bhakti
contemporary_relevance: Bhakti-Sufi traditions continue to influence India's composite cultural heritage.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Heterogamy sounds related to different maturation times due to its 'hetero' prefix, but it refers to the production of different types of gametes. Dichogamy specifically means temporal separation of maturation.
elimination_hint: Dichogamy = 'dicho' (apart) + 'gamy' (mating/maturation) — temporal separation of androecium and gynoecium maturation to prevent self-pollination; eliminate Herkogamy (spatial separation) and Heterogamy (different gamete types).
factual_hooks: Dichogamy: androecium and gynoecium mature at different times — prevents self-pollination
Protandry: stamens mature first; Protogyny: pistils mature first — both are types of dichogamy
contrast_with: Dichogamy (temporal) vs Herkogamy (spatial) — both prevent self-pollination but by different mechanisms

subject: History
trap_factor: The 'not correct' framing means three statements are correct; candidates may overlook that the Barisal agitation leader's identity requires specific knowledge, making statement A the incorrect one.
elimination_hint: The Barisal agitation (Swadeshi era) was led by Aswinikumar Dutta, not Laikat Hossain; this makes statement A incorrect, which is the answer.
factual_hooks: Bengal National College was founded in 1906 with Aurobindo Ghosh as Principal
Satish Chandra Mukherjee formulated national education scheme in 1906 (not 1989)
Tagore's Atmasakti philosophy focused on rural social and economic regeneration
contrast_with: Moderates vs Extremists — methods and objectives differed in Indian National Congress

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Candidates may think pitch differs because different instruments produce different pitches for the 'same note,' but pitch and frequency are the same; only quality (timbre) distinguishes instruments playing the same note.
elimination_hint: Same note = same pitch and frequency. Loudness can be the same. Only timbre/quality (due to different harmonics/overtones) distinguishes sitar from flute; option C is correct.
factual_hooks: Quality/timbre is determined by the number and intensity of overtones/harmonics
Same note on different instruments = same pitch and loudness (if played equally), but different timbre
contrast_with: Pitch (frequency) vs Quality (timbre) — often confused in sound questions

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Both A and R seem true at first glance — diamonds do sparkle more. The trap is in the Reason: diamond actually has a HIGHER refractive index than glass, not lower, making R false.
elimination_hint: Assertion A is true (diamond sparkles more due to total internal reflection). Reason R is false — diamond's refractive index (~2.42) is much higher than glass (~1.5). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Diamond refractive index ~2.42 vs glass ~1.5 — diamond is higher
Higher refractive index causes greater total internal reflection, enabling more sparkle
contrast_with: Total internal reflection vs refraction — TIR occurs when light goes from denser to rarer medium beyond critical angle

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse Article 13 (laws inconsistent with FR are void) as part of Right to Equality since it precedes it. Article 13 is a protective provision, not a right itself.
elimination_hint: Right to Equality spans Articles 14-18: Article 14 (equality before law), 15 (no discrimination), 16 (equal opportunity), 17 (abolition of untouchability), 18 (abolition of titles). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Right to Equality: Articles 14-18
Article 17 abolishes untouchability
Article 18 abolishes titles except military/academic
contrast_with: Article 13 (protective provision) vs Articles 14-18 (Right to Equality) — Article 13 enables enforcement of FRs but is not itself a right
contemporary_relevance: Right to Equality is foundational to anti-discrimination jurisprudence in India.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: The Doppler effect for light is counterintuitive — a receding source shifts light toward red (redshift), but at 2/10 light speed the shift may seem like it should reach orange or red, not yellow.
elimination_hint: Doppler redshift: blue light shifts toward longer wavelengths when source recedes. At 0.2c, blue shifts toward yellow/green range. Answer: C (Yellow).
factual_hooks: Doppler redshift: receding source shifts light to longer wavelengths (toward red)
Blue light (~450nm) shifts toward yellow/green range at moderate recession speeds
contrast_with: Redshift (recession) vs Blueshift (approach) — opposite Doppler effects for light

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: The statement uses inclusive 'or' (either...or), meaning at least one is true. Candidates may confuse inclusive vs exclusive 'or' — in logic, 'or' is inclusive unless stated as 'exclusive or'.
elimination_hint: With inclusive 'or': if one part is false, the other must be true (modus tollens). II (not lazy → stupid) and III (not stupid → lazy) are valid; option B (II and III) is correct.
factual_hooks: 'Either A or B' in logic allows both to be true (inclusive or)
Modus tollens: if not-B then A, from 'A or B'
contrast_with: Inclusive OR (at least one true) vs Exclusive OR (exactly one true)

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: The question involves tax savings from investment, not just interest; candidates may directly take 12% as the answer without accounting for the tax benefit that effectively increases the return.
elimination_hint: Tax saved = (30%-10%) × Rs.1000 = Rs.200/month additional benefit. Effective return = Rs.120 interest + Rs.200 tax saving on Rs.1000 = Rs.320/Rs.1000 = 32% but annualized on net investment gives effective rate of 20%. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Effective interest = nominal interest + tax saved due to investment
Tax arbitrage increases effective yield of long-term savings instruments
contrast_with: Nominal interest rate vs Effective interest rate — effective rate accounts for tax benefits

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse meteor with meteorite (reaches earth's surface) or asteroid (orbits sun); 'rapidly moving star' is a common misconception since meteors appear as shooting stars.
elimination_hint: A meteor is a piece of matter (rock/debris) from outer space that enters Earth's atmosphere and burns up; it is NOT a star. Meteorite is what survives to hit the ground. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Meteor: space matter entering Earth's atmosphere (burns up as 'shooting star')
Meteorite: meteor that survives and hits Earth's surface
Asteroid: rock orbiting the Sun
contrast_with: Meteor vs Meteorite — meteor burns in atmosphere; meteorite reaches Earth's surface

subject: Geography
trap_factor: The five Central Asian republics of the former Soviet Union have similar-sounding names and are geographically adjacent; without a map, positional identification is extremely difficult.
elimination_hint: Kazakhstan is the largest and most northern; Turkmenia borders Iran in the south; Uzbekistan is central; use the Aral Sea (shaded water body) as a geographic anchor to identify positions.
factual_hooks: Kazakhstan is the largest Central Asian republic, bordering Russia in the north
Turkmenia (Turkmenistan) borders Iran and Afghanistan in the south
contrast_with: Central Asian republics vs Caucasus republics — different geographic regions of former USSR

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: At 3:25, the minute hand is exactly at '5' (25 minutes = 150°). The hour hand is not at 3 but has moved 25/60 of the way from 3 to 4; candidates forget to account for the hour hand's partial movement.
factual_hooks: Hour hand moves 0.5° per minute; minute hand moves 6° per minute
At 3:25, hour hand is at 102.5°, minute hand at 150°
contrast_with: Clock problems vs direction problems — both involve angles but different setups

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Options A and B suggest South Africa sided with one ethnic group, which seems plausible given South Africa's own ethnic history; the correct answer involves a diplomatic/peacekeeping role.
elimination_hint: South Africa sent troops as a protection force to help mediate between warring groups (Hutu and Tutsi) in Burundi's civil war under Nelson Mandela's mediation initiative; answer D.
factual_hooks: South Africa deployed troops to Burundi in 2001 as part of Mandela's peace mediation initiative
Burundi civil war involved Hutu-Tutsi ethnic conflict similar to Rwanda
contrast_with: South Africa's role in Burundi (mediation) vs in DRC (peacekeeping under AU)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Sunderbans is the most famous mangrove ecosystem in India and may seem the obvious answer; however, the specific mention of palm cultivation, timber exploitation, and freshwater pocket drying uniquely fits Andaman & Nicobar.
elimination_hint: Clues: 'monoculture of commercially viable trees', 'palm cultivation', 'destruction of mangroves', 'freshwater pockets drying' all point to Andaman & Nicobar Islands, not Sunderbans. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Andaman & Nicobar Islands face threats from palm cultivation and illegal logging
Sunderbans is in West Bengal, not described by 'freshwater pockets drying up'
contrast_with: Sunderbans (Bengal delta mangrove) vs Andaman mangroves — different ecosystem contexts
contemporary_relevance: Andaman & Nicobar Islands remain a biodiversity hotspot under conservation pressure.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Leaf blight and leaf spot are also common plant diseases and may seem plausible; 'leaf rust' is a specific fungal disease (Hemileia vastatrix) that devastated Sri Lanka's coffee in the 1870s.
elimination_hint: Hemileia vastatrix (coffee leaf rust) destroyed Sri Lanka's coffee plantations in the 1870s, leading to the switch to tea cultivation. Answer: C (leaf rust).
factual_hooks: Coffee leaf rust (Hemileia vastatrix) devastated Sri Lanka's coffee in 1870s
Sri Lanka switched from coffee to tea after the leaf rust epidemic
contrast_with: Leaf rust (fungal) vs leaf blight (bacterial/fungal) — both foliar diseases but different pathogens

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: The question text appears garbled (options don't match the stated question about mirror rotation and reflected ray); the options about 'increase/decrease in size' suggest a different question about stellar objects may have been merged.
elimination_hint: Based on the law of reflection: when a mirror rotates by angle θ, the reflected ray rotates by 2θ. The correct answer C (maintain its size) seems to relate to a different question about stellar/celestial objects.
factual_hooks: Law of reflection: when mirror rotates by θ, reflected ray rotates by 2θ
This is a fundamental principle used in optical instruments like galvanometers
contrast_with: Plane mirror (rotates reflected ray by 2θ) vs curved mirror (different reflection geometry)

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates may draw Venn diagrams incorrectly by placing Carrot and Vegetable as overlapping circles instead of nested ones. Carrot is a subset of Vegetable which is a subset of Food.
elimination_hint: Carrot ⊂ Vegetable ⊂ Food — three concentric circles with Food outermost, Vegetable inside it, Carrot inside Vegetable. Select Venn diagram I showing this nested relationship.
factual_hooks: All carrots are vegetables; all vegetables are food — shows set inclusion
Nested Venn diagrams represent subset relationships
contrast_with: Overlapping circles (partial intersection) vs nested circles (full inclusion) in Venn diagrams

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Vivekananda believed moral strength of individuals underpins societal progress
He emphasized character-building and ethical conduct over legal compliance
contrast_with: Vivekananda (morality-based society) vs Bentham (law-based utilitarianism)
mains_intro: Vivekananda's quote highlights that formal laws are secondary to the internalized moral values of citizens and administrators.
mains_body_points: Laws are external constraints; morality is an internal compass — a truly ethical society needs both but morality is foundational
Relevance for civil servants: rule-following without ethical motivation leads to mechanical governance; ethical civil servants go beyond mere compliance
Contemporary context: rising corruption, institutional decay, and governance failures reflect weakening of moral fabric despite strong legal frameworks
Vivekananda's vision: character formation through education and values as the bedrock of national strength
mains_conclusion: Ultimately, sustainable and just governance emerges from ethically grounded individuals, not merely robust legal systems.
contemporary_relevance: Relevant in the context of ethical governance debates and anti-corruption discourse in India.

subject: History
trap_factor: All four terms sound like ancient Indian social/kinship terms; Kosa (treasury/sheath) is the odd one out as Kula (family), Vamsa (lineage), and Gotra (clan) are all kinship terms.
elimination_hint: Kula = family group, Vamsa = lineage/dynasty, Gotra = patrilineal clan — all kinship categories. Kosa = treasury/storehouse — belongs to political/economic vocabulary. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Kula, Vamsa, Gotra are kinship/lineage terms in ancient Indian society
Kosa means treasury — part of Saptanga theory of state, not kinship
contrast_with: Kosa (treasury, political concept) vs Gotra (clan, social concept)

subject: Society
trap_factor: Chapchar Kut sounds similar to other Northeast festivals; candidates may confuse it with festivals of Assam or Arunachal Pradesh.
elimination_hint: Chapchar Kut is the most important festival of Mizoram, celebrated in spring after the jungle is cleared for jhum (shifting) cultivation. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Chapchar Kut is Mizoram's most important spring festival
It celebrates completion of the most arduous part of jhum (shifting) cultivation
contrast_with: Chapchar Kut (Mizoram) vs Bihu (Assam) — both spring festivals but different states and cultures

subject: History
trap_factor: Saumilla and Sudraka are known Gupta-era literary figures; candidates may confuse them with medical scholars. Susrutha (Sushruta) is distinctly known for surgery but is associated with earlier or contemporaneous periods.
elimination_hint: Susrutha (Sushruta) authored Sushruta Samhita — the foundational text of Ayurvedic surgery — and is the correct answer. Shaunaka is a Vedic scholar. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Sushruta authored Sushruta Samhita — foundational text of Ayurvedic surgery
Charaka authored Charaka Samhita — foundational text of Ayurvedic medicine (earlier period)
contrast_with: Sushruta (surgery) vs Charaka (medicine/internal) — both Ayurvedic but different specializations
contemporary_relevance: Sushruta is recognized as a pioneer of plastic surgery globally.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Statement 1 about NTPC being largest power utility and statement 3 about IOC operating ALL oil refineries are the key traps — IOC does not operate all refineries (HPCL, BPCL, ONGC also operate refineries).
elimination_hint: Statement 3 is false (IOC doesn't operate all refineries) and Statement 2 is debatable; D (NTPC as largest power utility and Indian Ordnance Factory as largest departmentally run industrial undertaking) is most defensible.
factual_hooks: Indian Ordnance Factory is the largest departmentally-run industrial undertaking
NTPC was the largest power generating utility in India
contrast_with: NTPC (electricity) vs ONGC (oil & gas) — different energy sectors

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Oxidation number calculation requires knowledge of each compound's formula; candidates may misapply rules for peroxides (CaO2) where oxygen has -1 oxidation state, giving Ca +2, not +4.
elimination_hint: CaO2 is calcium peroxide — O is -1 each, so Ca is +2. MnO2: O is -2×2=-4, so Mn is +4. Match A-3(Ca=+2), B-4(Al=-3 in NaAlH4), C-1(Mn=+4), D-2(S=+6 in H2S2O7). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: In peroxides (like CaO2), oxygen has -1 oxidation state (not -2)
In H2S2O7 (pyrosulfuric acid), S has +6 oxidation state
contrast_with: Peroxide bond (O-O, each O at -1) vs normal oxide (O at -2)

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement C is the incorrect one — the All India Muslim League (formed 1906) actually SUPPORTED the partition of Bengal and separate electorates, not opposed them; this is a commonly tested reversal.
elimination_hint: Muslim League was formed partly to protect Muslim interests and SUPPORTED separate electorates (Morley-Minto reforms 1909) and did NOT oppose Bengal's partition. Statement C is incorrect. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Muslim League (1906) supported partition of Bengal and demanded separate electorates
Khudai Khidmatgars (Red Shirts) under Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan opposed British rule
Maulana Barkaullah established Provisional Government of India in Kabul (1915)
contrast_with: Muslim League (pro-partition, separate electorates) vs INC (anti-partition, joint electorates)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Candidates may remember that Lok Sabha term can be extended during emergency but forget the specific unit — it is one year at a time (not two years or six months at a time).
elimination_hint: Article 83(2): Lok Sabha can be extended by Parliament by law for one year at a time during National Emergency, for as long as the emergency is in force. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Lok Sabha term can be extended by one year at a time during National Emergency (Article 83)
Extension cannot exceed beyond the emergency period
contrast_with: Lok Sabha (can be dissolved/extended) vs Rajya Sabha (permanent, never dissolved)

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Candidates may exclude time deposits thinking they are not 'money'; M1 excludes time deposits but M3 (broad money) includes them as they can be converted to cash.
elimination_hint: M3 = M1 + time deposits with banks. M1 = currency with public + demand deposits + other deposits with RBI. All three components (1, 2, 3) are part of M3. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: M3 (Broad Money) = Currency with public + Demand deposits + Time deposits + Other deposits with RBI
M1 (Narrow Money) excludes time deposits; M3 includes them
prerequisite_concepts: Money supply aggregates (M0, M1, M2, M3)
contrast_with: M1 (narrow money, excludes time deposits) vs M3 (broad money, includes time deposits)
contemporary_relevance: RBI monitors M3 growth as an indicator of monetary expansion and inflation.

subject: History
trap_factor: Option A (union with Afghanistan) sounds plausible given Pashtun identity; option C (communist ideology) may attract candidates who know Khan was influenced by socialist ideas; the correct answer emphasizes Pashtun unity and anti-colonialism.
elimination_hint: Khudai Khidmatgars (Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan's 'Red Shirts') advocated Pathan/Pashtun regional nationalism and non-violent resistance against British colonialism — not union with Afghanistan or communism. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Khudai Khidmatgars = 'Servants of God'; Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan = 'Frontier Gandhi'
Red Shirts promoted non-violent Pashtun nationalism against British rule
contrast_with: Khudai Khidmatgars (non-violent) vs Chittagong Armory Raid (violent revolutionary movement)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Option A states all echinoderms are viviparous — many echinoderms (like sea urchins) are oviparous (egg-laying), so this is clearly incorrect and is the 'not correct' answer.
elimination_hint: Echinoderms (sea stars, sea urchins) are predominantly oviparous (egg-laying), not viviparous (live birth). All other statements (round worm lacking circulatory system, bony fish swim bladder, cartilaginous fish internal fertilization) are correct. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Echinoderms are oviparous (lay eggs), not viviparous
Round worms (nematodes) have no circulatory system — nutrients diffuse directly
contrast_with: Echinoderms (oviparous) vs sharks/cartilaginous fish (some viviparous/ovoviviparous)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Albedo is the reflectivity of a surface; candidates may confuse the order assuming snow-covered tundra has highest albedo — but tundra snow cover makes it highest, with taiga second due to dark conifer trees absorbing light.
elimination_hint: Albedo order (decreasing): Tundra (snow-covered, high reflectivity) > Taiga (some snow + dark conifers) > Tropical deciduous (seasonal leaf cover) > Tropical evergreen (dense dark canopy, lowest albedo). Answer: C (4,1,3,2).
factual_hooks: Tundra has highest albedo due to snow and ice cover
Tropical evergreen forests have lowest albedo due to dense dark canopy absorbing sunlight
contrast_with: High albedo (reflects sunlight, cooling effect) vs low albedo (absorbs sunlight, warming effect)
contemporary_relevance: Albedo changes due to deforestation and ice melt are key factors in climate change.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Great Nicobar being the southernmost island is well-known, causing candidates to incorrectly associate it with the highest peak; Saddle Peak is in the Andaman group (North Andaman), not Nicobar.
elimination_hint: Saddle Peak (732m) is the highest point in A&N Islands, located in North Andaman island, not Great Nicobar. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Saddle Peak (732m) is the highest point in Andaman & Nicobar Islands, located in North Andaman
Great Nicobar is the southernmost island of India, not the location of Saddle Peak
contrast_with: Saddle Peak (North Andaman) vs Indira Point (Great Nicobar, southernmost tip of India)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Goa's beaches are well-known, which may mislead candidates; Tamil Nadu also has turtle-nesting beaches. Odisha's Gahirmatha beach is the world's largest Olive Ridley nesting site.
elimination_hint: Odisha's (Orissa) Gahirmatha Marine Sanctuary and Rushikulya rookery are the largest Olive Ridley sea turtle nesting sites in the world. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Gahirmatha Beach, Odisha = world's largest mass nesting site for Olive Ridley turtles
Olive Ridley turtles travel from South America and elsewhere to nest at Odisha coast
contrast_with: Olive Ridley (Odisha nesting) vs Leatherback (different species, nests at different locations)
contemporary_relevance: Olive Ridley conservation is an ongoing concern due to fishing net entanglement and coastal development.

subject: History
trap_factor: Both A and R may appear correct; the camp 'Swarga-dwari' is an obscure fact, and both the desertion of Delhi and a plague connection are historically documented, making the assertion-reason link seem valid.
elimination_hint: Muhammad bin Tughlaq did leave Delhi for Swarga-dwari, and it was indeed due to a plague-like epidemic; both A and R are true and R explains A. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Muhammad bin Tughlaq left Delhi for Swarga-dwari camp due to an epidemic/plague
Muhammad bin Tughlaq is known for eccentric decisions including Delhi-to-Daulatabad capital shift
contrast_with: Muhammad bin Tughlaq (Tughluq dynasty, 1325-51) vs Alauddin Khilji (Khilji dynasty)

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Both A and R are individually true, but R (shoreline stabilization) is the result of mangrove characteristics, not the reason why they are specialized ecosystems of tropical/subtropical coasts — the two are related but R doesn't explain why mangroves exist where they do.
elimination_hint: A is true: mangroves are indeed specialized ecosystems of tropical/subtropical coastal regions. R is also true: they do stabilize shorelines. But R does not explain why they are specialized coastal ecosystems — the reason is their adaptation to saline, tidal, anaerobic conditions. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Mangroves grow in tropical/subtropical intertidal zones with saline-tolerant adaptations (pneumatophores, prop roots)
They stabilize shorelines but this is an ecological service, not the reason for their coastal specialization
contrast_with: Mangroves (saline coastal) vs freshwater wetlands — different adaptations and locations
contemporary_relevance: Mangrove conservation is critical for coastal resilience against cyclones and sea-level rise.

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Climate change is anthropogenic — driven by industrial emissions, deforestation, overconsumption
Intergenerational equity demands present generations limit resource exploitation
contrast_with: Anthropocentric (human-centered) vs Ecocentric (ecosystem-centered) ethics on environment
mains_intro: Climate change reflects a fundamental ethical failure — the prioritization of short-term material gain over long-term planetary health and justice.
mains_body_points: Root cause: industrial greed, consumerism, and political short-termism driving greenhouse gas emissions and ecological destruction
Ethical dimension: current generations are depleting resources at the cost of future generations — violation of intergenerational equity
Solutions: transition to renewable energy, circular economy, sustainable agriculture, and carbon pricing create equilibrium between society and environment
Role of governance: international cooperation (Paris Agreement), domestic policy (renewable targets), and behavioral change driven by ethical leadership
Individual-collective responsibility: lifestyle changes + systemic change both needed
mains_conclusion: Ending the exploitation-extinction spiral requires a paradigm shift from growth-at-any-cost to a values-driven, sustainable relationship between humanity and the Earth.
contemporary_relevance: India's NDC targets and COP commitments make this directly relevant to current affairs.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Candidates may think the Chief Justice of India supervises all High Court CJs or that the Governor has administrative control; the correct answer involves the unique principle that CJ's administrative acts are subject to judicial review by fellow judges.
elimination_hint: When the Chief Justice of a High Court acts administratively, their actions are subject to the writ jurisdiction of other judges of the same High Court. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: CJ of HC in administrative capacity is subject to writ jurisdiction of other HC judges
This reflects the principle of judicial accountability even within the judiciary
contrast_with: CJ of India's supervisory role (Article 235) over subordinate courts vs HC CJ's administrative accountability

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse Hawala with stock market manipulation or commission payments; the defining feature of Hawala is informal cross-border money transfer bypassing official banking channels.
elimination_hint: Hawala = informal value transfer system where money is received in one country's currency and paid in another's without physical money crossing borders or going through official banking. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Hawala is an informal remittance system based on trust and a network of brokers (hawaladars)
Hawala transactions bypass official banking channels and currency controls — illegal in India
contrast_with: Hawala (informal, illegal transfer) vs SWIFT (formal, official international transfer)
contemporary_relevance: Hawala remains a key mechanism for terror financing and money laundering globally.

subject: Economy
factual_hooks: India's Green Energy Corridor aims to evacuate renewable energy from renewable-rich states to demand centers
It involves upgrading transmission infrastructure to handle variable renewable generation
contrast_with: Conventional energy grid (baseload, stable) vs smart/green grid (variable renewable integration)
mains_intro: India's Green Energy Corridor is a transformative infrastructure initiative to integrate large-scale renewable energy into the national grid.
mains_body_points: Objective: evacuate solar and wind energy from renewable-rich states (Rajasthan, Gujarat, Tamil Nadu, AP) to deficit regions
Components: inter-state and intra-state transmission lines, substations, and smart grid technologies to handle intermittent renewable supply
Implementing agencies: Power Grid Corporation of India (PGCIL) for inter-state; state transmission utilities for intra-state
Significance: reduces curtailment of renewable energy, improves grid stability, and reduces dependence on coal-based power
Challenges: land acquisition for transmission lines, financing, coordination among states
mains_conclusion: The Green Energy Corridor is central to India achieving its renewable energy targets and transitioning to a low-carbon economy.
contemporary_relevance: Directly relevant given India's 500 GW renewable target by 2030 and energy transition goals.

subject: History
trap_factor: Mathura sculptures are also known for their use of specific stone (spotted red sandstone); candidates may confuse the Gandhara-green schist link or assume Bharhut sculptures used schist.
elimination_hint: Gandhara sculptures, influenced by Greco-Roman traditions, characteristically used grey/green schist (phyllite) as medium, giving them their distinctive look. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Gandhara sculptures used grey/green schist — distinctive Greco-Roman influenced Buddhist art
Mathura school used spotted red sandstone; Amaravati used white marble
contrast_with: Gandhara (schist, Hellenistic style) vs Mathura (sandstone, indigenous style) — two major schools of Buddhist art

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates may calculate P(green) = 25/60 and then subtract from 1, but may make arithmetic errors; the straightforward calculation is (30+5)/60 = 35/60 = 7/12.
elimination_hint: P(not green) = P(yellow) + P(red) = 5/60 + 30/60 = 35/60 = 7/12. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Complementary probability: P(A') = 1 - P(A)
contrast_with: Complementary probability vs conditional probability — different concepts

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Mars Climate Orbiter (1998) and Mars Global Surveyor (1996) are also NASA Mars missions launched around the same period, causing confusion with the 2001 launch.
elimination_hint: Mars Odyssey was launched in April 2001 and remains NASA's longest-operating Mars spacecraft. Mars Climate Orbiter was 1998 (failed). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Mars Odyssey launched April 7, 2001 — NASA's 2001 Mars mission
Mars Odyssey detected hydrogen (possible water ice) near Mars south pole
contrast_with: Mars Odyssey (2001, orbiter) vs Mars Pathfinder (1996, lander/rover)

subject: History
trap_factor: The Rajagopalachari Formula and Wavell Plan also failed but the Cabinet Mission Plan (1946) is specifically considered the last real opportunity for a united India before partition became inevitable.
elimination_hint: The Cabinet Mission Plan (1946) proposed a united India with a three-tier federal structure; its rejection by both Congress and League made partition inevitable. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Cabinet Mission Plan (1946) was the last proposal for a united India with a federal structure
Both INC and Muslim League's rejection/acceptance disagreements led to its failure
contrast_with: Cabinet Mission (federal united India) vs Mountbatten Plan (partition) — sequential and contrasting outcomes

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement A (one-third area gets >750mm rainfall) may seem correct since India has diverse rainfall, but actually MORE than one-third gets >750mm; the dominant irrigation source being wells is also counterintuitive.
elimination_hint: Statement A is incorrect — more than one-third (roughly two-thirds) of India records >750mm rainfall annually. Groundwater/wells are indeed the dominant irrigation source. Answer: A (not correct statement).
factual_hooks: Wells/groundwater is the dominant irrigation source in India (~60-65% of irrigated area)
Mountain areas account for roughly 30% of India's surface area
contrast_with: Canal irrigation (dominant in north) vs well irrigation (dominant nationally overall)

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates must set up two equations: one for full immersion with 0.2kg mass, another for floating 2cm above water without mass. Confusing which condition applies to which equation is the main trap.
elimination_hint: Let side = a cm. Volume of cube = a³ cm³. When 2cm above water, submerged volume = a²(a-2). Buoyancy = a²(a-2)g = a³×ρ_cube×g. With 0.2kg: a³ρ_cube×g + 0.2×1000g = a³g → solving gives a = 10cm. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Archimedes principle: buoyancy = weight of fluid displaced
When cube floats 2cm above water: buoyancy equals weight of cube
contrast_with: Fully submerged (complete immersion) vs partially submerged (floating) — different buoyancy calculations

subject: History
trap_factor: Options A and D seem plausible as reasons for a Muslim historian to question Islamic credentials; the actual reason (sultans supplemented Sharia with Zawabits — royal decrees) requires specific knowledge of Barani's critique.
elimination_hint: Barani's key objection was that Delhi Sultans (especially Alauddin Khilji) created Zawabits — regulations supplementary to Muslim law — undermining the primacy of Sharia. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Barani was the historian-author of Tarikh-i-Firuz Shahi and Fatawa-i-Jahandari
Delhi Sultans used Zawabits (royal regulations) alongside Sharia, which Barani considered un-Islamic
contrast_with: Theocratic ideal (Sharia supreme) vs sultanate practice (royal regulations alongside Sharia)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Candidates may select option B (2, 3, 4) thinking condensation is not needed for thunderclouds, or option A thinking only condensation and humidity matter without vertical wind.
elimination_hint: Thundercloud (cumulonimbus) formation requires all four: high temperature and humidity (energy source), vertical winds (lifting mechanism), orography (triggering uplift), and condensation (cloud formation). Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Cumulonimbus (thundercloud) formation requires strong vertical (convective) currents
All four factors — condensation, high T&H, orography, vertical wind — contribute to thundercloud development
contrast_with: Orographic rainfall (stable uplift) vs convective thunderstorms (intense vertical motion)
contemporary_relevance: Increasing thunderstorm frequency linked to climate change is a contemporary concern.

subject: CSAT
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse the chain of percentage calculations — 10% rebate on marked price, 15% profit on cost price; working backwards: SP = Cost × 1.15, and SP = Marked Price × 0.90.
factual_hooks: If SP after 10% discount = Rs.82.8, marked price = 82.8 ÷ 0.9 = Rs.92
Profit % is always calculated on Cost Price, not Marked Price
contrast_with: Profit on CP vs markup on CP — both affect final selling price differently

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Natural selection is often considered the primary driver of evolution; mutation provides genetic variation and sexual reproduction increases it, but isolation is the crucial mechanism that allows new species to form (speciation).
elimination_hint: Isolation (geographic/reproductive) prevents gene flow between populations, allowing natural selection and mutation to drive divergence into new species. Without isolation, gene flow prevents speciation. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Geographic isolation is the most important factor in allopatric speciation
Without isolation, gene flow between populations prevents divergence into new species
contrast_with: Isolation (prevents gene flow, enables speciation) vs mutation (provides variation but alone insufficient for speciation)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Sessions judge and chief presidency magistrate are both judicial officers that might seem included in 'district judge'; the Constitution excludes tribunal judges from this definition.
elimination_hint: Article 236 defines 'district judge' to include sessions judge, chief presidency magistrate, chief judge of small cause court, etc., but NOT tribunal judges (who have a separate statutory basis). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Article 236: 'district judge' includes sessions judge, additional district judge, chief presidency magistrate, etc.
Tribunal judges are appointed under separate Acts and are NOT covered by the term 'district judge' in the Constitution
related_concepts: High Court jurisdiction over subordinate courts
contrast_with: Courts (under Constitution's Chapter V) vs Tribunals (statutory, under separate Acts) — different appointment and oversight mechanisms

subject: History
trap_factor: The name Mudrarakshasa contains 'rakshasa' which may suggest demonic or mythological content; the play is actually about political intrigue at Chandragupta's court, not mythology.
elimination_hint: Mudrarakshasa by Visakhadatta is a Sanskrit play about the political machinations of Chanakya (Kautilya) in installing and securing Chandragupta Maurya's throne. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Mudrarakshasa = 'The Signet Ring of the Rakshasa (minister)' — political drama about Chandragupta's court
Visakhadatta (Vishakhadatta) was the playwright; Chanakya is the protagonist
contrast_with: Mudrarakshasa (political drama) vs Mricchakatika (Shudraka) — social romantic drama of a different era

subject: Geography
trap_factor: La Nina (opposite of El Nino) is associated with stronger monsoons, not weaker ones. Candidates may confuse La Nina with El Nino's effects on Indian monsoon.
elimination_hint: El Nino and Southern Oscillations (ENSO) warming the Pacific leads to weakened Indian monsoon. La Nina has opposite effect (stronger monsoon). Answer: C.
factual_hooks: El Nino warms the eastern Pacific, weakening the pressure differential that drives Indian monsoon
La Nina cools the eastern Pacific, typically strengthening the Indian monsoon
contrast_with: El Nino (warm, weak monsoon) vs La Nina (cool, strong monsoon) — opposite ENSO phases
contemporary_relevance: ENSO monitoring is critical for agricultural planning and disaster preparedness in India.

subject: History
trap_factor: Kalibangan and Banawali both have connections to ploughed fields and terracotta ploughs — candidates may swap these. Dholavira's large signboard inscription is a distinctive but less-known fact.
elimination_hint: Lothal = Dockyard (A-2), Kalibangan = Ploughed field (B-1), Dholavira = Large inscription signs (C-4), Banawali = Terracotta plough replica (D-3). Answer: B (A-2, B-1, C-4, D-3).
factual_hooks: Lothal had a dockyard — unique trade facility in Harappan civilization
Dholavira had a large inscription of 10 signs of Harappan script
Kalibangan had the earliest evidence of a ploughed field
contrast_with: Lothal (coastal trade, dockyard) vs Mohenjo-daro (inland urban center) — different Harappan site functions

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Options A (larger outlay) and B (agricultural thrust) are plausible 'differences' of the 8th Plan; the critical distinguishing feature is the abolition of industrial licensing under LPG reforms, which began exactly with the 8th Plan.
elimination_hint: The 8th Five-Year Plan (1992-97) coincided with LPG (Liberalization, Privatization, Globalization) reforms; industrial licensing was abolished — a paradigm shift distinguishing it from earlier plans. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: 8th Five-Year Plan (1992-97) abolished industrial licensing as part of LPG reforms
New Industrial Policy 1991 dismantled the License Raj during 8th Plan period
contrast_with: 7th Plan (License Raj era) vs 8th Plan (post-liberalization) — fundamental policy shift

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Candidates may place Agriculture as largest contributor (as a common stereotype) or Manufacturing as second. In 2000-01, Trade/Hotels/Transport contributed more than Manufacturing to GDP.
elimination_hint: Decreasing order of GDP contribution (2000-01): Services (Trade, Hotels, Transport) > Financing/Insurance/Real Estate > Agriculture > Manufacturing gives 3, 4, 1, 2. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: India's GDP in 2000-01 was dominated by services sector
Trade, Hotels, Transport & Communication was the largest services component
contrast_with: India (services-led growth) vs China (manufacturing-led growth) — different development paths

subject: History
trap_factor: Gomateshwara statue at Shravanabelagola is the tallest Jain statue — students may confuse which Tirthankara it represents. It represents Bahubali (son of first Tirthankara Rishabhanatha), not the last (24th) Tirthankara Mahavira.
elimination_hint: Statement A is incorrect — Gomateshwara (Bahubali) was not a Tirthankara at all; he was the son of Rishabhanatha (1st Tirthankara). The statue represents Bahubali's meditation. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Gomateshwara = Bahubali, son of Rishabhdev (1st Tirthankara) — NOT any Tirthankara
Mahavira was the 24th (last) Tirthankara of Jainism
contrast_with: Bahubali (Gomateshwara) vs Mahavira — both Jain figures but different roles

subject: History
trap_factor: Pavarana sounds like it could be about initiation (taking robes) due to 'pava' prefix; candidates may confuse it with Pabbajja (novice ordination) or Uposatha ceremony.
elimination_hint: Pavarana was held at the end of Vassa (rainy season retreat); monks confessed offences committed during the retreat period. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: Pavarana = end-of-Vassa confession ceremony in Buddhist monastic tradition
Vassa is the rainy season retreat lasting ~3 months for monks
contrast_with: Pavarana (confession at end of Vassa) vs Uposatha (fortnightly recitation of Vinaya rules)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: WIPO's headquarters is Geneva (not Rome — that's FAO/WFP). Also, least developed countries had a 10-year transition period under TRIPS, not 20 years, making Statement 4 incorrect.
elimination_hint: Statement 2 (WIPO HQ at Rome) is false — WIPO is in Geneva. Statement 4 is false — LDCs had 10-year (extended further) transition under TRIPS. Only Statements 1 and 3 are correct. Answer: D (1 and 3).
factual_hooks: WIPO headquarters is in Geneva, Switzerland (not Rome)
TRIPS Agreement is binding on all WTO members; LDCs had 10-year (not 20-year) transition period
contrast_with: WIPO (UN body, Geneva) vs FAO/WFP (Rome) — different UN specialized agencies and locations
contemporary_relevance: TRIPS flexibilities for LDCs and COVID-19 vaccine IP waivers remain highly relevant.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement B (blood cells = 70% of blood volume) is wrong — blood cells are ~45% (hematocrit). Statement C (WBC made only by lymph nodes) is wrong — bone marrow makes most WBCs. Statement D (more platelets than WBC) is the correct one.
elimination_hint: Platelets: 150,000-400,000 per µL; WBC: 4,000-11,000 per µL — platelets vastly outnumber WBCs. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Platelets (1.5-4 lakh/µL) greatly outnumber WBCs (4,000-11,000/µL)
Hematocrit (blood cell volume) is ~45%, not 70%
contrast_with: Platelets (thrombocytes, clotting) vs WBC (leukocytes, immunity) — different functions and counts

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Candidates may think silicon is dominant in Earth's composition (it's second most abundant in crust), or that the Sun contains 99% of solar system mass. Earth IS the densest planet in the solar system.
elimination_hint: Earth is the densest planet (5.5 g/cm³). Iron is Earth's most abundant element by mass (not silicon). The Sun contains ~99.8% of solar system mass (not 75%). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Earth is the densest planet in the solar system at ~5.5 g/cm³
Iron is Earth's most abundant element by mass (not silicon, which dominates the crust only)
contrast_with: Earth (densest) vs Saturn (least dense, would float on water)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement about pulses being 90% rainfed is the correct one but may seem too high; India does account for a large share of world pulse area but the rice percentage (15% of world area) and gross cropped area share are often misstated.
elimination_hint: About 90% of India's pulse area is indeed rainfed — pulses grow in dry, rainfed regions. India accounts for ~25-30% of world rice area (not 15%). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: ~90% of India's pulse cultivated area is rainfed
India is the world's largest producer and consumer of pulses
contrast_with: Pulses (mostly rainfed, dry land) vs Rice (requires intensive irrigation in many regions)
contemporary_relevance: India's pulse production challenges continue to affect food security and farmer income.

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Candidates often confuse this with Supreme Court judges (charged to Consolidated Fund of India). High Court judges' salaries are charged to the Consolidated Fund of the STATE, not India.
elimination_hint: Article 202(3): High Court judges' salaries and allowances are charged to the Consolidated Fund of the respective State. Supreme Court judges are charged to Consolidated Fund of India. Answer: B.
factual_hooks: HC judges' salaries → Consolidated Fund of the State (Article 202)
SC judges' salaries → Consolidated Fund of India (Article 112)
prerequisite_concepts: Consolidated Fund of India vs State
contrast_with: SC judges (CFI) vs HC judges (CFS) — different funds despite both being 'charged' (not voted)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Both statements may seem true: glycerine viscosity does change with temperature and kinetic energy does increase with temperature. However, Assertion A is FALSE — glycerine's viscosity DECREASES with increasing temperature (like all liquids).
elimination_hint: Liquids (including glycerine) decrease in viscosity as temperature rises (molecules move faster, intermolecular forces weaken). Gases increase in viscosity with temperature. A is FALSE, R is TRUE. Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Liquids: viscosity DECREASES with temperature (intermolecular forces weaken)
Gases: viscosity INCREASES with temperature (more molecular collisions)
contrast_with: Liquid viscosity (decreases with T) vs gas viscosity (increases with T) — opposite trends

subject: History
trap_factor: Both A and R seem plausible: labour participation was weak, and communist ideological differences existed. The trap is that R accurately explains A — communist/socialist unions kept distance from the 'bourgeois' Congress.
elimination_hint: Labour participation in the Civil Disobedience Movement (early 1930s) was indeed weak (A true); Communist leaders criticized INC as representing capitalist interests (R true and explains A). Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Communist Party of India viewed INC as bourgeois and did not support Civil Disobedience Movement
Labour unions under communist influence stayed away from Gandhi's movements in early 1930s
contrast_with: Nationalist labour (supporting INC movements) vs communist labour (ideologically opposed to INC)

subject: History
trap_factor: Baba Farid and Saiyid Muhammad Gesudaraz are also famous Chishti saints associated with devotion; the founder of the Chishti order in India (Muin-ud-din Chishti) specifically popularized devotional music (Sama/Qawwali).
elimination_hint: Khwaja Muin-ud-din Chishti of Ajmer is the founder of the Chishti Sufi order in India and specifically championed devotional music (Sama) as a path to God. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Muin-ud-din Chishti founded Chishti order in India; popularized Qawwali/Sama as devotional music
Chishti order is known for its emphasis on music (Sama) unlike the Naqshbandi order which opposed it
contrast_with: Chishti (music-accepting) vs Naqshbandi (music-rejecting) Sufi orders
contemporary_relevance: Qawwali tradition and Sufi shrines remain important elements of India's composite culture.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Both A and R are true and causally linked, making this an assertion-reason question where both being true AND R explaining A is the correct combination, which candidates may overlook.
elimination_hint: Synthetic detergents do lather in hard water (A true) BECAUSE they form soluble calcium/magnesium salts (unlike soaps which form insoluble scum with hard water). R correctly explains A. Answer: A.
factual_hooks: Synthetic detergents have sulfonate groups that form soluble Ca/Mg salts — work in hard water
Soaps form insoluble Ca/Mg salts (scum) in hard water — don't lather well
contrast_with: Soaps (natural, insoluble with hard water salts) vs detergents (synthetic, soluble with hard water salts)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Both A and R may seem true — the sub-polar low pressure belt IS at 60-65° latitudes. However, the Reason given (low pressure permanent over oceans rather than land) misidentifies the cause; the real cause is polar front/convergence, not ocean vs land.
elimination_hint: A is TRUE: 60-65° latitudes have sub-polar low pressure. R is FALSE: the reason is polar front convergence of warm tropical and cold polar air masses, not the claim about oceans vs land. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Sub-polar low pressure at 60-65° is caused by polar front where warm and cold air masses converge
The low is NOT because low pressure is 'permanent over oceans rather than land'
contrast_with: Sub-polar low (dynamic, due to convergence) vs equatorial low (thermal, due to heating)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Candidates may think State Legislative Councils are excluded from both processes; actually they are excluded from BOTH (not part of electoral college and not part of impeachment). The question asks what is part of electoral college but NOT impeachment.
elimination_hint: State Legislative Assemblies are part of President's electoral college (Article 54) but NOT part of the impeachment process (Article 61 — only Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha participate). Answer: D.
factual_hooks: Presidential election electoral college: elected MPs + elected MLAs (State Legislative Assemblies)
Presidential impeachment: only Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha — State Assemblies excluded
contrast_with: Electoral college (includes MLAs) vs impeachment forum (only Parliament — LS and RS)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Northern India (Punjab, Haryana) is associated with cotton historically but the majority of cotton production is in Western and Southern India (Maharashtra, Gujarat, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana).
elimination_hint: Western India (Gujarat, Maharashtra) and Southern India (AP, Telangana, Karnataka) produce the largest amount of cotton in India — black cotton soil (Regur) regions. Answer: C.
factual_hooks: Black cotton soil (Regur) in Deccan and Western India is ideal for cotton cultivation
Maharashtra and Gujarat are among the largest cotton producing states
contrast_with: Punjab (wheat, Green Revolution) vs Maharashtra/Gujarat (cotton) — different crop specializations

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Statement IV (promoting insecticides/pesticides) sounds like a government programme, but the National Forest Policy 1988 emphasizes natural regeneration and ecological balance — not chemical inputs.
elimination_hint: National Forest Policy 1988 includes afforestation (I), reforestation (II), and fuel substitutes (III) but NOT promotion of insecticides/pesticides (IV) — that contradicts its ecological philosophy. Answer: D (I, II and III).
factual_hooks: National Forest Policy 1988: goal to maintain 33% of land under forests
Policy emphasizes social forestry, JFM (Joint Forest Management), not chemical pest control
contrast_with: National Forest Policy 1952 (industrial/commercial focus) vs 1988 (ecological/social focus)
contemporary_relevance: Forest conservation remains central to India's climate commitments and biodiversity goals.

subject: History
trap_factor: Varahamihira is known for both astronomy AND astrology (Brihat Samhita); candidates may confuse him with mathematics. Brahmagupta is a mathematician, not an astronomer, though he did work in astronomy.
elimination_hint: Visakhadatta (Drama — Mudrarakshasa), Varahamihira (Astronomy — Panchasiddhantika, Brihat Samhita), Charaka (Medicine — Charaka Samhita), Brahmagupta (Mathematics — Brahmasphutasiddhanta). Answer: C (I-B, II-C, III-A, IV-D).
factual_hooks: Varahamihira: Panchasiddhantika (astronomy) and Brihat Samhita (encyclopedic)
Brahmagupta: Brahmasphutasiddhanta — introduced concept of zero and negative numbers
contrast_with: Varahamihira (astronomy/astrology) vs Brahmagupta (mathematics) — often confused Gupta scholars
